methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 5 same-sector + same-stage peers in AI & Software. Range: $500M - $32.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

private.name: Safe Superintelligence
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series B), post-money $32B

private.name: Physical Intelligence
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series B), post-money $5.6B

private.name: World Labs
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series B), post-money $5B+

private.name: Nominal
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series B), post-money $1B

private.name: Photoroom
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series B), post-money $500M

data.narrative:
Reality Defender is a Series B-stage AI & Software company founded by Ben Colman and Gaurav Bharaj that has raised $50M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable universe of 5 same-sector, same-stage private peers (Safe Superintelligence, Physical Intelligence, World Labs, Nominal, and Photoroom) spanning a range of $500M to $32.0B, plus 1 public comparable.

Two triangulation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis uses the $50M total raised as a proxy base value and applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B advancement, implying a valuation of $95M. Peer-median analysis identifies a median valuation of $5.0B across the 5-company peer set, with valuations ranging from $500M (Photoroom) to $32.0B (Safe Superintelligence). This wide dispersion reflects heterogeneity in business model and investor appetite within the frontier AI sector.

Triangulation of these two methods yields a low estimate of $81M, a median of $2.5B, and a high of $5.8B. The wide range reflects meaningful uncertainty about whether Reality Defender's market position and growth trajectory are closer to lower-valuation peers or to mid-tier competitors like Physical Intelligence ($5.6B) and World Labs ($5.0B). Key uncertainties include revenue run-rate, customer concentration, and path to profitability relative to the peer set.
